metadata.description: We performed Pavlovian cue-concurrent transcranial focused ultrasound stimulation (tFUS) stimulation during intracranial single-neuron recordings using a wearable array transducer targeting the nucleus accumbens (NAc) in awake and behaving rats.   The stimulation was delivered with a 64-element array transducer. Experimental parameters included pulse repetition frequency (PRF), tone burst duration (TBD), total sonication time, and input voltage to Vantage ultrasound system (can be converted to pressure level). For example, the notation mou05_50_50_50_5 indicates the animal number, PRF (Hz), input voltage (v), TBD (microseconds), and total sonication time (sec), respectively.

name: Individual variability of neural computations underlying flexible decisions

metadata.name: Individual variability of neural computations underlying flexible decisions
metadata.citation: Pagan, Marino; Tang, Vincent D.; Aoi, Mikio C.; Pillow, Jonathan W.; Mante, Valerio; Sussillo, David; Brody, Carlos D. (2026) Individual variability of neural computations underlying flexible decisions (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001550/draft
metadata.description: In this study rats were trained to perform a task requiring context-dependent selection and accumulation of sensory evidence. The task was performed in a behavioural box consisting of three straight walls and one curved wall with three nose ports. Each nose port was equipped with an LED to deliver visual stimuli, and with an infrared beam to detect the rat's nose when entering the port. In addition, above the two side ports there were speakers to deliver sound stimuli, and water cannulas to deliver a water reward. At the beginning of each trial, rats were presented with an audiovisual cue indicating the context of the current trial, either prevalent location (LOC) context or prevalent frequency (FRQ) context. The context cues consisted of 1-s-long, clearly distinguishable frequency modulated sounds, and in addition the LOC context was signalled by turning on the LEDs of all three ports, whereas in the FRQ context only the centre LED was turned on. After the end of the context cue, the rats were required to place their nose into the centre port. While maintaining fixation in the centre port, rats were presented with a 1.3-s-long train of randomly timed auditory pulses. Each pulse was played either from the speaker to the rat's left or from the speaker to their right, and each pulse a 5-ms pure tone with either low frequency (6.5 kHz) or high frequency (14 kHz). The pulse trains were generated by Poisson processes with different underlying rates. The strength of the location evidence was manipulated by varying the relative rate of right versus left pulses, and the strength of the frequency evidence was manipulated by varying the relative rate of high versus low pulses. The overall pulse rate was kept constant at 40 Hz. In the LOC context, rats were rewarded if they turned, at the end of the stimulus, towards the side that had played the greater total number of pulses, ignoring the frequency of the pulses. In blocks of frequency trials, rats were rewarded for orienting left if the total number of low-frequency pulses was higher than the total number of high-frequency pulses, and orienting right otherwise, ignoring the location of the pulses. The context was kept constant in blocks of trials, and block switches occurred after a minimum of 30 trials per block, and when a local estimate of performance reached a threshold of 80% correct.

relatedResource.name: Individual variability of neural computations underlying flexible decisions

name: Migroglia to neuron signaling affects neuronal network activity

metadata.name: Migroglia to neuron signaling affects neuronal network activity
metadata.citation: Cline, Hollis (2025) Migroglia to neuron signaling affects neuronal network activity (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001557/draft
metadata.description: Microglia regulate neuronal circuit plasticity. Disrupting their homeostatic function has detrimental effects on neuronal circuit health. Neuroinflammation contributes to the onset and progression of neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease (AD), with several microglial activation genes linked to increased risk for these conditions. Inflammatory microglia alter neuronal excitability, inducing metabolic strain. Interestingly, expression of APOE4, the strongest genetic risk factor for AD, affects both microglial activation and neuronal excitability, highlighting the interplay between lipid metabolism, inflammation, and neuronal function. It remains unclear how microglial inflammatory state is conveyed to neurons to affect circuit function and whether APOE4 expression alters this intercellular communication. Here, we use a reductionist model of human iPSC-derived microglial and neuronal monocultures to dissect how the APOE genotype in each cell-type independently contributes to microglial regulation of neuronal activity during inflammation. Conditioned media (CM) from LPS-stimulated microglia increased neuronal network activity, assessed by calcium imaging, with APOE4 microglial CM driving greater neuronal activity than APOE3 CM. Both APOE3 and APOE4 neurons increase network activity in response to CM treatments, while APOE4 neurons uniquely increase presynaptic puncta in response to APOE4 microglial CM. CM-derived exosomes from LPS-stimulated microglia can mediate increases to network activity. Lastly, increased network activity is accompanied by increased lipid droplet (LD) metabolism and blocking LD metabolism abolishes network activity. These findings illuminate how microglia-to-neuron communication drives inflammation-induced changes in neuronal circuit function, demonstrate a role for neuronal LDs in network activity, and support a potential mechanism through which APOE4 increases neuronal excitability.

name: LFP Recordings in Macaque During Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Neuromodulation

metadata.name: LFP Recordings in Macaque During Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Neuromodulation
metadata.citation: Yeh, Kelly (2025) LFP Recordings in Macaque During Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Neuromodulation (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001558/draft
metadata.description: In this study, we performed simultaneous recordings of intracranial local field potentials (LFP) and EEG in a macaque monkey implanted with a chronic electrode system during transcranial focused ultrasound stimulation (tFUS). Ultrasound was delivered using a 128-element array transducer targeting multiple brain regions including auditory cortex (A1), somatosensory cortex (S1), and the thalamus (VPLc). All recordings were conducted in an awake, passively resting animal.  Each session includes alternating active and sham trials. In active trials, ultrasound was delivered using parameterized pulse trains with varied pulse repetition frequency (PRF: 40 Hz, 300 Hz, 1500 Hz, 3000 Hz) and stimulation amplitude (estimated in situ pressure 15 - 255 kPa). Sham trials were interleaved by redirecting ultrasound to a collimator. Ultrasound duration was fixed at 300 ms, and auditory cue (white noise) if delivered was at 200 ms post-tFUS-onset for 25 ms. Raw electrophysiology data includes broadband LFP recordings from around 800 channels across 14 coronal slices and 33-channel EEG data acquired simultaneously, concatenated into [N_channels × N_timepoints (ms) × N_trials].

name: Dual Enkephalinase Inhibitor Effects on Stress-Induced Reward Seeking Neural and Behavioral Deficits

metadata.name: Dual Enkephalinase Inhibitor Effects on Stress-Induced Reward Seeking Neural and Behavioral Deficits
metadata.citation: Harris, Alexander (2025) Dual Enkephalinase Inhibitor Effects on Stress-Induced Reward Seeking Neural and Behavioral Deficits (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001563/draft
metadata.description:
This dataset contains the raw data from behavioral and fiber photometry recording sessions for the first phase of a study investigating the effects of a dual enkephalinase inhibitor on stress-induced deficits in reward-seeking behavior in mice. The data is organized into individual session folders, each containing the files from a single experimental session. Each session folder is named with the following convention: [animal_id]_[session_type]_[session_day]_[YYYYMMDD]_[HHMMSS]. Data Structure within each Session Folder: Each session folder contains a set of synchronized files from different recording systems:

task_data.mat / test_data.mat: A MATLAB .mat file containing the primary trial-structured behavioral data from the task control software. It is structured into two main variables:
sessionInfo: Contains session-level metadata such as animalID, testDay, date, injectionStatus, stressStatus, etc.
trialData: A structure containing an array of trials. Each trial in the array is a structure with the following key fields:
trialNum: The trial number.
cueTime: The absolute timestamp (from the behavioral system's clock) of the auditory cue onset.
rewardTime: The absolute timestamp of the reward delivery.
ITIDuration: The duration of the inter-trial interval for that trial.
responded: A boolean flag (0 or 1) indicating if the animal successfully licked to receive a reward.
rewardSize: A scalar value indicating the volume or amount of reward delivered on that trial.
monitoring_data.mat: A MATLAB .mat file containing continuous, high-temporal-resolution data streams from the behavioral monitoring system. This file is not trial-structured and contains raw sensor readings and synchronization signals, including:
A continuous, high-frequency raw lick sensor signal.
A TTL signal (sound_ttl) that goes high during the presentation of the auditory cue for precise event marking.
A TTL signal (fp_ttl) sent from the fiber photometry system that goes high for the duration of the recording, used for synchronizing the two systems.
FPdata[...].csv: A CSV file containing the raw data from the fiber photometry system. Each row is a single sample, with the following columns:
ComputerTimestamp: The timestamp (in milliseconds) from the photometry computer's clock.
LedState: An integer indicating which LED was active for that sample (e.g., 1 for 470nm, 2 for 410nm).
G0: The raw fluorescence value recorded from the photodetector for that channel and timestamp.
video[...].avi: A video file recording the animal's behavior during the session.
video_timestamps[...].csv: A CSV file containing the precise timestamp for each frame acquired from the camera, as recorded by the camera control computer. This file typically contains a single column of timestamps in milliseconds.

Synchronization Method: The different data streams are synchronized using a shared TTL pulse and precise timestamping.

The fiber photometry system generates a TTL pulse (fp_ttl) that is recorded by the behavioral monitoring system. The rising and falling edges of this pulse mark the exact start and end of the photometry recording on the behavioral clock.
The video_timestamps[...].csv provides a timestamp for each video frame on the camera's computer clock. The number of timestamps is matched against the total number of frames in the .avi file to ensure data integrity.
By aligning the start of the fp_ttl pulse from the behavioral data with the first timestamp in the FPdata CSV, and using the sound_ttl to align behavioral events, all data streams (trial events, neural activity, and video) can be precisely aligned in a common time base for analysis.

Code Availability: The Jupyter notebooks and Python modules used to load, synchronize, and analyze this data are available at the following GitHub repository: https://github.com/HarrisNeuralCircuitsLab/Dual-Enkephalinase-Inhibitor-Stress-Effect

metadata.citation: Ros, Ivo; Dickinson, Michael; Dickson, William; Omoto, Jaison; Wang, Ruoxi (2025) Preliminary two-photon calcium imaging data for 1R01NS136988-01: The functional role and neural basis of rapid turns in olfactory search behavior (Version 0.250901.0248) [Data set]. keywords: flight, central complex,  mechanosensation, GLNO, calcium imaging
metadata.description: Calcium imaging of putative members of the pathway that relays visual and mechanosensory information to the compass neurons in the central complex
studyTarget: We explore the role of mechanosensation in accurately estimating self-rotation during rapid flight saccades.  Like mammalian head-direction cells, flies update their heading representation via GLNO neurons that relay idiothetic sensory information to EPG compass neurons within the central complex. We functionally imaged left and right GLNO neuron activity in the fly brain, alongside recordings of wingstroke amplitudes during mechanical and visual stimulations at different simulated rotation speeds. Two-photon microscopy recordings use GCaMP7f, a Ca2+-indicator, alongside an activity-independent red fluorophore, tdTomato. tdTomato is used for image-stabilization and GCaMP-fluorescence normalization.

metadata.description: This Dandiset contains session-wise NWB files for in vivo two-photon calcium imaging in head-fixed mice navigating an auditory virtual environment (Funamizu A, Kuhn B, Doya K (2016). Neural substrate of dynamic Bayesian inference in the cerebral cortex. Nature Neuroscience, 19, 1682-1689. https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.4390). Imaging targeted posterior parietal cortex (PPC) and adjacent posteromedial cortex (PM) across layers 2/3 and 5 while animals ran on a spherical treadmill in darkness with continuous or intermittent sound feedback. The dataset includes segmented ROI masks and population activity (raw ratio and ΔF/F) as RoiResponseSeries, together with aligned behavioral signals (locomotion/position, forward–lateral speed, lick rate, sound on/off indicators, and reward events) stored under the behavior module. Sampling is uniform at ~30.9 Hz for imaging and behavior, enabling trial-level analyses of neural dynamics during goal-directed navigation. Please cite the above article when using this dataset.
studyTarget: How dynamic Bayesian inference is realized in the cortical circuit

metadata.description: Rats transfected with a viral strategy to express a light-activated channel (ChR2) in PV+ neurons within the MS of PV-Cre transgenic rats. We also implanted an optic fiber targeting the MS as well as either silicon electrodes or multi-tetrode arrays targeting the hippocampus. Each of these animals received optical stimulation restricted to periods of locomotion on either a lineartrack, a W-shaped track, or both. The stimulation pattern was governed by a protocol that either activated MS PV neurons at a wide range of requested frequencies (“rhythmic stimulation”) or at specific phases of the ongoing theta rhythm (“phase-specific stimulation”) recorded in the hippocampus. Released in conjunction with the publication "Disruption of theta-timescale spiking impairs learning but spares hippocampal replay".

metadata.description: Comparative neuron morphology dataset of thermosensory projection neurons (TPNs) from 2-photon microscopy data of dextran dye-filled neurons from Drosophila melanogaster, D. mojavensis, D. persimilis, and D. mettleri brains. In this project, we labelled the TPNs in the brain that relay heat information from the periphery (antennae) to the central brain (lateral horn and calyx of the mushroom body) through dextran dye filling. Included in this dataset are the following: Ten z-stack images of the labelled projection neuron called TPN-V (which is the main TPN for innocuous heat) per species, with five acquired in the "Front view" and five in the "Top view", respectively; Front and Top view fills of TPN-V for an additional population strain of each species; Representative fills of other TPN cell types (1a, 1b, and 6) labelled in D. mojavensis. See Capek, et al. (2025) for more details: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-08682-z.

metadata.description: This dataset includes 50 recording sessions of intracranial EEG and simultaneous eyetracking from 46 individual patients. Implants consist of a total of 10,769 contacts predominantly from depth electrodes, and 986 from ECoG contacts (grids and strips). Patients watched up to 53.6 minutes of movies (48 recordings), completed resting state (25 recordings) and viewed static images (31 recordings). Data is provided in NWB format and includes local field potentials, electrode locations, gaze and pupil size and precise timing of each frame in the movie. The data also includes annotations of words and phonemes in one of the movies, as well as annotions of faces in three movies.

name: In vivo electrophysiology recordings from the ventral hippocampus of anesthetized rats

metadata.name: In vivo electrophysiology recordings from the ventral hippocampus of anesthetized rats
metadata.citation: Colarusso, Olivia (2025) In vivo electrophysiology recordings from the ventral hippocampus of anesthetized rats (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001622/draft
metadata.description:
This dataset contains raw and partially processed electrophysiological recordings collected from the hippocampus of adult rats under anesthesia. Recordings were acquired using multi-channel silicon probes and the Open Ephys acquisition system, stored in .dat format. The dataset spans multiple sessions capturing spontaneous neural activity under stable anesthetic conditions.

Initial spike sorting and analysis have been performed using SpikeInterface-compatible tools. Future versions of this dataset may include NWB-formatted files, enhanced metadata, and annotations related to probe placement, anesthesia protocol, and signal quality metrics.

This draft is intended for internal collaboration and pipeline development. Metadata and structure are subject to revision.

metadata.description: Electrophysiology recordings from primary motor cortex (M1) of macaque monkeys (Macaca mulatta) during visuomotor step-tracking tasks, collected before and after MPTP-induced parkinsonism. Recorded neuron types: Pyramidal tract neurons (PTNs) and corticostriatal neurons (CSNs) identified through antidromic stimulation from chronically implanted electrodes in the cerebral peduncle and posterior putamen. Behavioral paradigm: Visuomotor step-tracking task requiring flexion or extension elbow movements to align a cursor with visual targets. Trials included center hold, target presentation, movement execution, target hold, and liquid reward delivery. Unexpected torque perturbations were applied during a subset of trials to elicit stretch reflexes.

name: Micro-ECoG pseudoword speech repetition

metadata.name: Micro-ECoG pseudoword speech repetition
metadata.citation: Cogan, Gregory (2025) Micro-ECoG pseudoword speech repetition (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001638/draft
metadata.description: This enclosed data is collected using high-density, high channel-count micro-electrocorticography (micro-ECoG) neural recordings from awake neurosurgical patients in the operating room. Arrays used were either 256-channel grids (1.72 mm electrode spacing), or 128-channel strips (1.33 mm electrode spacing). Patients repeated pseudowords that were either one syllable (consonant-vowel-consonant) or two syllables (vowel-consonant-vowel), from a total inventory of 9 different phonemes. Task duration was approximately 10-15 minutes. The data were collected at the Duke University Medical Center and approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Duke University Health System (Pro00072892). This dataset is curated by Drs. Gregory Cogan and Jonathan Viventi and is formatted in the BIDS standard with continuous data that contains both auditory markings and response markings for tokens and phonemes. No audio data is uploaded for HIPAA compliance. Anatomical locations of each electrode are also included.
metadata.acknowledgement: Funding Source: NIH R01DC019498

metadata.citation: Taniguchi, Masachika (2025) Neural activity in rat nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum during probabilistic free-choice decision-making (Ito & Doya 2009) (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001641/draft
metadata.description: This Dandiset contains NWB files with behavioral and extracellular electrophysiological recordings from six male Long-Evans rats performing a conditional free-choice task with probabilistic rewards. In the task, rats maintained a center nose-poke during an auditory cue and then chose left or right nose-pokes; reward probabilities for each action switched across blocks according to four (left, right) schedules: (90,50), (50,90), (50,10), and (10,50) percent. Single-unit activity was recorded using movable microwire bundles implanted in the nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum across 70 sessions (≈60,000 trials in total). For each trial, the dataset includes spike times, trial type (choice vs no-choice), selected action, reward outcome, and key event timestamps (tones, nose-pokes, pellet retrieval), all aligned within session time. These data were originally collected for Ito & Doya, J Neurosci 2009 (DOI: 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.6157-08.2009), which compared reinforcement-learning models of behavior and analyzed how action values, trial type, and reward outcome are represented in ventral basal ganglia. This dataset is suitable for re-analysis of decision-making strategies, model-based neural coding, and computational studies of cortico-basal ganglia circuits.

metadata.description:
This Dandiset contains Neurodata Without Borders (NWB) files with behavioral and extracellular electrophysiological recordings from seven male Long–Evans rats performing fixed-choice and free-choice nose-poke tasks with probabilistic rewards. In each trial, rats initiated a center nose-poke, received one of three cue tones (left, right, or choice), maintained the poke during the cue, and then chose the left or right port; sucrose pellets were delivered according to cue- and action-dependent reward probabilities. For left and right cues, reward probabilities were fixed at (left, right) = (50%, 0%) and (0%, 50%), respectively, whereas for the choice cue they varied blockwise across four schedules: (90%, 50%), (50%, 90%), (50%, 10%), and (10%, 50%). 
Neural activity was recorded with movable microwire bundles implanted in dorsolateral striatum (DLS), dorsomedial striatum (DMS), and ventral striatum (VS), yielding phasically active neurons (putative medium spiny neurons) from each subregion across 86 sessions and ~78,000 trials. For each session, the NWB files provide spike times (and associated unit metadata where available) together with detailed behavioral event timestamps (tone onsets/offsets, nose-poke entries/exits, reward/no-reward tones, pellet detection), block type (single fixed-choice, double fixed-choice, free-choice, extinction), cue identity, chosen action, and reward outcome, all aligned in session time. These data were originally collected for the Journal of Neuroscience article “Distinct neural representation in the dorsolateral, dorsomedial, and ventral parts of the striatum during fixed- and free-choice tasks” (Ito & Doya, 2015, J Neurosci 35(8):3499–3514, DOI: 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.1962-14.2015), which analyzed how state value, action value, reward outcome, and action commands are differentially represented across striatal subregions. This dataset enables re-analysis of decision-making strategies, model-based and model-free value coding, and hierarchical reinforcement-learning hypotheses in cortico-basal ganglia circuits.

name: Multi-electrode array recordings of rat retinal ganglion cells with LED stimulation for retinotectal cell identification via ReaChR phototagging

metadata.name: Multi-electrode array recordings of rat retinal ganglion cells with LED stimulation for retinotectal cell identification via ReaChR phototagging
metadata.citation: Rudzite, Andra (2025) Multi-electrode array recordings of rat retinal ganglion cells with LED stimulation for retinotectal cell identification via ReaChR phototagging (Version 0.251202.2310) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://doi.org/10.48324/dandi.001677/0.251202.2310
metadata.description:
Retinal tissue was obtained from Long-Evans rats that received stereotactic injections of AAV2retro-CAG-ReaChR-mCitrine into the superior colliculus. This retrograde viral approach resulted in expression of the red-shifted channelrhodopsin ReaChR and the fluorophore mCitrine in retinotectal RGCs.
Ex vivo retinal recordings were performed on 512-electrode arrays following pharmacological blockade of photoreceptor-mediated responses using L-AP4, DL-AP5, and CNQX. LED flashes at 565 nm were delivered at multiple intensities and durations (10, 30, 100, and 200 ms) to selectively activate ReaChR-expressing cells. The dataset contains recordings from 6 experiments, each capturing responses from hundreds of RGCs to the LED stimulation protocol.

name: Data for parametrizing alternating current stimulation for neuromodulation (Bell Vila, M., et al, 2026)

metadata.name: Data for parametrizing alternating current stimulation for neuromodulation (Bell Vila, M., et al, 2026)
metadata.citation: Bell Vila, Monica; Koletar, Margaret; Dorr, Adrienne; Trevisiol, Andrea; Goubran, Maged; Sled, John G.; Bazzigaluppi, Paolo; Stefanovic, Bojana (2026) Data for parametrizing alternating current stimulation for neuromodulation (Bell Vila, M., et al, 2026) (Version 0.260227.1824) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://doi.org/10.48324/dandi.001678/0.260227.1824
metadata.description:
Data collected using a 4x4 multielectrode array (Microprobes) in propofol anesthetized rats receiving subcutaneous alternating current stimulation.

Recording electrodes had platinum iridium shanks with a tip width of 125μm, an impedance of 0.4 MOhm, and were spaced 0.5mm apart in the lateral plane. An additional shank with 10 kOhm impedance served as a reference. Electrodes were inserted ~300μm into the craniotomy exposed sensorimotor cortex (L2/3). Data was acquired with a sampling rate of 24 kHz (Tucker-Davis Technologies).

Stimulation electrodes were adhered directly to the skull, medial and lateral the cranial window.



There are two data files.

The first contains experiments where 9 three-month old Sprague-Dawley rats received a randomized order of stimulation parameters (frequencies: 5, 10, 20, 40, and 140 Hz, amplitudes: 50, 100, 200, and 400 μA). Each dated folder contains recordings for a single rat, with subfolders for each stimulation parameter delivery (1-min baseline + 1-min sACS + 1-min baseline).

The second data file contains experiments where 13 rats received a single 20-min delivery of 40 Hz, 100 μA stimulation. Each dated folder contains experiments from a single rat. Baseline activity was recorded for 20-min before stimulation onset and subsequently following sACS for 10 minutes. Additional subfolders contain further 20-min of post stimulation baseline activity, collected to an hour following stimulation offset.

name: Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Stimulation Targeting White Matter Inhibits Seizures in a Rat Model of Epilepsy

metadata.name: Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Stimulation Targeting White Matter Inhibits Seizures in a Rat Model of Epilepsy
metadata.citation: Gao, Huan; Frake, Annabel; Durand, Dominique M; He, Bin (2026) Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Stimulation Targeting White Matter Inhibits Seizures in a Rat Model of Epilepsy (Version 0.260109.2104) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://doi.org/10.48324/dandi.001683/0.260109.2104
metadata.description: In this dataset, we recorded 32-channel EEG and 64-channel intracranial signals from wild-type male rats and male rats with seizures induced by repeated 4-AP injections to investigate the effects of white-matter-targeted transcranial focused ultrasound (tFUS) on seizure inhibition. The 4-AP cocktail was injected into the right S1, and intracranial recordings were obtained from the left S1. The "labeled data" include seizure onset and offset times for each seizure, which were used to compare the tFUS and sham (no tFUS) conditions for each rat. Each rat has data from 4–5 sessions, each lasting approximately one hour: BASELINE (pre-tFUS or pre-sham), condition (CC_1Hz or sham), post1 (first hour post-stimulation), post2 (second hour post-stimulation), and post3 (third hour post-stimulation).tFUS was delivered using a 128-element transducer targeting the corpus callosum (CC, predominantly white matter) with a 1 Hz pulse repetition frequency (PRF), 2% duty cycle (DC), and ~127 kPa peak pressure. The "EP data" contain evoked potentials used to verify accurate targeting of the stimulation site with 1500Hz PRF, 30% DC and 67ms ultrasound duration (UD). The "EEG data for PLV" includes the EEG data for connectivity analysis of wild-type rats. Detailed description of this dataset can be found in the following publication. Please cite the paper if you would use a portion of the dataset. H. Gao, A. Frake, D. M. Durand and B. He, "Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Stimulation Targeting White Matter Inhibits Seizures in a Rat Model of Epilepsy," in IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering, vol. 34, pp. 251-259, 2026, doi: 10.1109/TNSRE.2025.3644273

name: RESOLVE: high-Resolution Electrode System fOr LGN Visual Enhancement - Dr. Lan's Lab

metadata.name: RESOLVE: high-Resolution Electrode System fOr LGN Visual Enhancement - Dr. Lan's Lab
metadata.citation: Sun, Yingchu (2025) RESOLVE: high-Resolution Electrode System fOr LGN Visual Enhancement - Dr. Lan's Lab (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001685/draft
metadata.description:
RESOLVE: High-Resolution Electrode System for LGN Visual Enhancement is a neuroengineering research project led by Dr. Lan Luan’s laboratory at Rice University. The goal of RESOLVE is to investigate how high-density, ultraflexible neural electrode systems can be used to record from and stimulate the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) to understand visual encoding and to inform the development of next-generation visual prostheses.

The project integrates in vivo electrophysiology, electrical microstimulation, behavioral assays, and optical imaging in animal models to study LGN neural dynamics during visual stimulation and artificial activation. Experiments generate multi-channel neural recordings, stimulation event data, behavioral responses (including eye movements), and imaging-based measurements such as retinotopic maps.

Data generated under RESOLVE include raw electrophysiological recordings, stimulation and behavioral event logs, and associated metadata describing experimental conditions and acquisition parameters. By combining high-resolution neural interfaces with systematic analysis and open data sharing, RESOLVE aims to advance fundamental understanding of thalamic visual processing and accelerate the translation of LGN-based visual prosthetic strategies.

name: Retrograde transduction of dopaminergic cells in substantia nigra of rhesus monkey

metadata.name: Retrograde transduction of dopaminergic cells in substantia nigra of rhesus monkey
metadata.citation: Plotnikova, Anya (2025) Retrograde transduction of dopaminergic cells in substantia nigra of rhesus monkey (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001686/draft
metadata.description: Neuromodulatory systems regulate neural circuits across broad regions of the brain, and disruption of the dopaminergic system contributes to psychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders. Engineered viral vectors have been used to target the neuromodulatory systems of the nonhuman primate brain (Y. Chen et al., 2023; El-Shamayleh et al., 2016; Gray et al., 2010; Lerchner et al., 2014; Perez et al., 2022). However, a conspicuous obstacle to the isolation and modulation of specific pathways is the inability of many retrogradely infecting viruses to transduce dopaminergic (DA) cells efficiently (Tervo et al., 2016; Cushnie et al., 2020; Weiss et al., 2020). We compare the DA neuron retrograde transduction efficacy of four viral vectors after injection into the striatum of nonhuman primates (NHP). Selectivity was assessed by comparing the neuronal co-expression of fluorescent reporter protein and tyrosine-hydroxylase (TH) antibody in substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc). The rabies pseudotyped lentiviral vector, FuGB2, produced superior retrograde transduction of DA cells to FuG-C or FuG-E. AAV2.retro was the least effective.

metadata.description: This Dandiset contains 3 high-resolution EMG recordings (16-channel bipolar) from rat triceps brachii along with synchronized 3D motion tracking for 10 keypoints during treadmill locomotion. The motion tracking was acquired at 125Hz using 4 BlackFly S cameras (BFS-U3-16S2M-CS) and labeled with DeepLabCut. The calibration and triangulation was performed with Anipose and this Dandiset contains the 3D points outputted in the pose-3d folder, along with the confidence values. A small proportion of these values (outliers) were interpolated by the cubic spline method if they had a confidence below 0.8 or if crossing a jump threshold of 6 pixels compared to a 7-frame median-filtered history (i.e., score_threshold=0.8, medfilt=7, offset_threshold=6, see Anipose documentation). The interpolation of a time point is represented by a NaN value for the confidence score of a given keypoint. No further filtering was performed on the 3D pose estimates. Along with these, this Dandiset also contains labels for the initial contact and initial swing times for the forelimb palm of the implanted side (left; i.e., palm_L).

name: An anatomical hotspot for striatal dopamine-acetylcholine interactions during reward and movement: multi-fiber photometry data

metadata.name: An anatomical hotspot for striatal dopamine-acetylcholine interactions during reward and movement: multi-fiber photometry data
metadata.citation: Vu, Mai-Anh; Bouabid, Safa; Noggle, Christian; Vietti-Michelina, Stefania; Brimblecombe, Katherine; Platt, Nicola; Zhang, Liangzhu; Joshi, Anil; Cragg, Stephanie; Howe, Mark (2026) An anatomical hotspot for striatal dopamine-acetylcholine interactions during reward and movement: multi-fiber photometry data (Version 0.260303.1724) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://doi.org/10.48324/dandi.001692/0.260303.1724
metadata.description: Dopamine (DA) and acetylcholine (ACh) are key neuromodulators that regulate striatal circuits underlying movement and reinforcement learning. Evidence suggests that DA and ACh systems interact, but where and how interactions are expressed across striatal regions in behaviorally relevant release dynamics remains unknown. We applied micro-fiber arrays to simultaneously measure striatum-wide DA and ACh in behaving mice, revealing an anatomical organization in which DA-ACh anti-correlations were concentrated in a hotspot in the anterior dorsolateral striatum (aDLS). Anti-correlations resulted from temporally coincident pairs of spontaneous and event-locked transient peak and dip events occurring in a DA-to-ACh sequence. The aDLS localized hotspot was consistently expressed within distinct signals associated with unpredicted rewards, learned and extinguished Pavlovian cues, and locomotion initiation and invigoration phases, for which we revealed novel, opposing DA-ACh dynamics. Optogenetic activation of DA neurons selectively suppressed spontaneous ACh release within the aDLS hotspot, and ex vivo recordings revealed enhanced D2-mediated ACh inhibition in aDLS relative to ventral regions, suggesting a mechanistic basis for this spatial specificity. These findings demonstrate that DA-ACh interactions during behavior are spatially organized, rather than uniformly conserved, and shape behaviorally relevant dynamics to regulate region-specific functions in learning and movement control.

metadata.description:
Overview This dataset provides high-density, simultaneous extracellular recordings from the mouse hippocampal formation (DG, CA1, CA2, CA3) and the retrosplenial cortex (RSC). The data captures large-scale neural dynamics across homecage rest, spatial navigation, and sensory stimulation.

Methodology:
Probes: Recordings were performed using Neuropixels 2.0 (CA3, CA1, RSC) and 1024-channel SiNAPS probes (DG, CA3, CA2, CA1).
Behavioral Paradigms: Neuropixels sessions include PRE-rest, Maze navigation (Novel or 2-maze configurations), and POST-rest homecage recordings. A subset of animals includes head-fixed odor stimulation.
SiNAPS sessions include High-density head-fixed recordings.

Data Included:
Sorted isolated units (Spikes).
Local Field Potentials (LFP), specifically including single-channel traces from ripple-detection sites.
Behavioral tracking variables (position, velocity, sleep periods, sharp-wave ripple times).

name: Loss of oxytocin receptor function disrupts neural signatures of pair bonding and fidelity in the nucleus accumbens

metadata.name: Loss of oxytocin receptor function disrupts neural signatures of pair bonding and fidelity in the nucleus accumbens
metadata.citation: Hoglen, Nerissa (2026) Loss of oxytocin receptor function disrupts neural signatures of pair bonding and fidelity in the nucleus accumbens (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001700/draft
metadata.description: The formation of enduring relationships dramatically influences future behavior, promoting affiliation between familiar individuals. The neural mechanisms of how such attachments are encoded to elicit and reinforce specific social behaviors in distinct ethological contexts remain understudied. Signaling via the oxytocin receptor (Oxtr) in the nucleus accumbens (NAc) facilitates social reward as well as pair bond formation between mates in socially monogamous prairie voles. How Oxtr function influences activity in the NAc during pair bonding to promote affiliative behavior with partners and rejection of other potential mates has not been determined. Using in vivo fiber photometry across pair bond formation and social attachment behaviors, we demonstrate that genetic loss of Oxtr function sex-specifically disrupts pair bonding behaviors and associated activity in the NAc. In females, loss of Oxtr influences prosocial behavior in a bonding-state-dependent manner. Oxtr-null females demonstrate reduced prosocial behaviors and lower activity in the NAc during initial chemosensory investigation of novel males. Following bond formation, affiliative behavior with partners and neural activity in the NAc during these interactions increase in an Oxtr-independent manner. Conversely, males lacking Oxtr display increased prosocial investigation of novel females. Altered behavioral and neural activity patterns in Oxtr-null males during their first interactions with females predict their future preference for a partner or stranger days later. These findings reconcile prior pharmacological and genetic studies. We demonstrate that disrupting Oxtr function sex-specifically influences the early development of pair bonds by modulating prosociality and the neural processing of sensory cues and social interactions with novel individuals, unmasking underlying sex differences in the neural pathways regulating the formation of long-term relationships.

name: Hippocampal CA2 Perineuronal Net Degradation and Social Memory Deficits in a 5XFAD Alzheimer’s Disease Mouse Model

metadata.name: Hippocampal CA2 Perineuronal Net Degradation and Social Memory Deficits in a 5XFAD Alzheimer’s Disease Mouse Model
metadata.citation: Sontheimer, Harald; Escalante, Miguel; Fleischel, Erik (2026) Hippocampal CA2 Perineuronal Net Degradation and Social Memory Deficits in a 5XFAD Alzheimer’s Disease Mouse Model (Version 0.260216.2323) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://doi.org/10.48324/dandi.001702/0.260216.2323
metadata.description: This dataset contains raw and processed experimental data supporting the findings reported in Chaunsali et al. (2025), Alzheimer’s & Dementia, DOI:10.1002/alz.70813. The study investigates the disruption of perineuronal nets (PNNs) in the hippocampal CA2 region of the 5XFAD Alzheimer’s disease mouse model and its association with selective loss of social cognition memory. Included are: Immunohistochemical imaging data quantifying PNN integrity in CA2 neurons, behavioral assay results assessing social memory performance and bulk RNA-sequencing data from hippocampal CA2 tissue with associated metadata.These data provide mechanistic insights into how PNN degradation correlates with specific forms of memory loss in neurodegenerative disease models and can be reused for comparative studies of extracellular matrix remodeling, Alzheimer’s disease pathology, and memory circuits. All data have been organized in compliance with FAIR principles to support reuse in neuroscience research and NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy requirements. Transcriptomic data associated with this study are deposited separately in the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) under accession GSEXXXXX and are linked here as a related resource. Transcriptomic data associated with this study are deposited separately in the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) under accession GSE317499 and are linked here as a related resource.

name: The direct regulation of feeding-driving hypothalamic GABAergic neurons on stress responses

metadata.name: The direct regulation of feeding-driving hypothalamic GABAergic neurons on stress responses
metadata.citation: Xu, Yuanzhong (2026) The direct regulation of feeding-driving hypothalamic GABAergic neurons on stress responses (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001705/draft
metadata.description: An appropriate stress response is essential for proper responding to, coping with, and subsequently recover from disturbing environmental stressors. However, the dysregulated responses to stress stimuli can result in abnormal psychiatric and behavioral outcomes, and if severe, can lead to stress-related mental disorders such as the posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). To develop effective therapies for treating these disorders, it is of utmost importance to fully understand the brain neural bases and mechanisms regulating stress responses. Emerging evidence indicates that stress-related emotional modalities are equally implicated in feeding regulation by hypothalamic neurons, suggesting that common neurocircuits co-regulate stress and feeding. Our tracing studies indicate that non-AgRP GABAergic neurons in the arcuate nucleus (Arc, denoted as ArcGABA neurons) send direct inputs to corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) neurons in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus (PVH, denoted as PVHCRH neurons). While PVHCRH neurons exhibit time-locked activation in response to various environmental stressors, we found that both GABA release onto PVHCRH neurons and ArcGABA neuron activity decrease under chronic stress conditions (e.g., tail suspension and restraint), but not under acute stressors (e.g., water spray and air puff). Specifically, ArcGABA neuronal activity correlates with GABA release onto PVHCRH neurons, but it is anticorrelated with PVHCRH neuronal activity in response to the same stressor. Furthermore, chemogenetic inhibition of PVHCRH-projecting ArcGABA neurons enhances PVHCRH neuronal activity and elevates stress levels, phenocopying the direct activation of PVHCRH neurons. Conversely, chemogenetic activation of PVHCRH-projecting ArcGABA neurons reduces both PVHCRH neuronal activity and stress levels, and this phenotype can be completely abolished by concurrent excitation of PVHCRH neurons. Altogether, our findings indicate that non-AgRP ArcGABA neurons play a critical role in encoding the scalability of stress responses to environmental stressors by modulating inhibitory GABA release onto PVHCRH neurons in a stimulus strength-dependent manner.
studyTarget: Mice

metadata.description: This dataset contains in vivo electrophysiological recordings and simultaneous behavioral data from male mice in NWB (Neurodata Without Borders) format.   Recordings were obtained during a reward competition task.  See the publication for protocol details:  Padilla-Coreano, N., Batra, K., Patarino, M. et al. Cortical ensembles orchestrate social competition through hypothalamic outputs. Nature 603, 667–671 (2022).  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04507-5.   Electrophysiological neural recordings were taken in the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC), mPFC projections to BLA, mPFC projections to LH.  The mPFC recordings contain "ephys" in filename; the mPFC to BLA contain “BLA” in file name, and mPFC to LH contain “chrimson” in the filename. Animals followed a consistent dye pattern across cages of 4 mice to identify them in behavioral videos.  Animal 1 was dyed on left side of body.  Animal 2 was dyed on right side of body.  Animal 3 was dyed with three horizontal stripes along body.  Animal 4 was dyed around the neck only.   Note that the animal id is different from animal social rank; please see subject description for social rank information. Raw behavioral videos are also included for the BLA and chrimson files.  Animal pose tracking was performed with AlphaTracker and required manual  correction of animal identities. Identity-corrected AlphaTracker pose estimates are included in this dataset.  Additionally, nine categories of  animal behavioral data, as described in Figure 1f of the reference paper, was hand scored for each trial from -2 s before tone onset to +12 seconds  after tone onset.  Note that there is an offset between the start of the neural recording and the start of the behavioral videos; this information  in contained in the video_gap variable in the behavioral events.  In the behavioral videos, the LED is on whenever the CS-tone is played and can be  used to verify the correct alignment between neural and behavioral data. Metadata includes approximate age, sex, species, strain, social rankings  of each animal and competitor (assessed with tube test), and port entries during reward competition task.  Not every subject in the dataset has  their competitor’s session included; those that do are noted in the subject description.

measurementTechnique.name: multi electrode extracellular electrophysiology recording technique

metadata.description: This dataset contains extracellular recordings from hippocampal place cells in three freely moving rats during the Neurolab Space Shuttle mission (STS-90) in April 1998. The experiment investigated whether the hippocampus can maintain stable spatial representations during three-dimensional navigation in microgravity. Rats were implanted with multi-electrode recording arrays and trained to traverse a three-dimensional track called the "Escher staircase," in which three 90-degree yaw turns were interleaved with three 90-degree pitch turns, creating a closed loop after only 270 degrees of total yaw rotation. This design placed the spatial information from external landmarks in conflict with the expected input from the head-direction system, which normally requires 360 degrees of yaw to signal a return to the starting direction. The dataset includes both preflight recordings on a flat rectangular track and in-flight recordings on the Escher staircase track from flight days 4 and 9. Place cells eventually demonstrated normal spatially specific firing, with spatial information content comparable to preflight levels, though initial exposures on flight day 4 showed abnormal spatial selectivity patterns. Hippocampal EEG was also recorded, showing normal theta rhythm during locomotion and normal sharp waves and ripples during quiet rest. Rats received medial forebrain bundle stimulation as a reward for traversing the track.

name: Novel voltage-dependent Cl− channels in striatal medium spiny neurons are unrelated to ClC-1 or other known Ca2+-induced Cl− channel/transporter types

metadata.name: Novel voltage-dependent Cl− channels in striatal medium spiny neurons are unrelated to ClC-1 or other known Ca2+-induced Cl− channel/transporter types
metadata.citation: Yarotskyy, Viktor; Contois, Liangru; Hahn, Yun-Kyung ; Nass, Sara R; Knapp, Pamela E; Hauser, Kurt F; National Institute on Drug Abuse (2026) Novel voltage-dependent Cl− channels in striatal medium spiny neurons are unrelated to ClC-1 or other known Ca2+-induced Cl− channel/transporter types (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001755/draft
keywords: Chloride channel, ClC-1, Medium spiny neurons, striatum, dissociated neurons
metadata.description: Intracellular chloride (Cl−) homeostasis is a critical regulator of neuronal excitability. Voltage-dependent neuronal Cl− channels remain the least understood in terms of their role as a source of Cl− entry controlling excitability. We have shown recently that striatal medium spiny neurons (MSNs) express a functional Cl− conducting ClC-1-like channel with properties similar but not identical to native ClC-1 channels (Yarotskyy, V., Lark, A.R.S., Nass S.R., Hahn, Y.K., Marone, M.G., McQuiston, A.R., Knapp, P.E., Hauser, K.F. (2022) Am. J. Physiol. Cell. Physiol. 322 (2022) C395-C409). Using a myotonic SWR/J-Clcn1adr-mto/J mouse model with a premature stop codon for the ClC-1 channel rendering it non-functional, we demonstrate that striatal MSNs isolated from wild type (wt) and homozygous mutant (adr) mouse embryos have identical voltage-dependent outwardly rectifying Cl− currents. In contrast and as expected, homozygous adr skeletal muscle flexor digitorum brevis (FDB) fibers display nominal macroscopic Cl− currents compared to heterozygous wild-type adr FDB fibers. Together, our findings demonstrate that the novel ClC-1-like channels in MSNs are unrelated to skeletal muscle-specific ClC-1 channels, and therefore represent a unique voltage-dependent neuronal Cl− channel of unknown identity.
studyTarget: Striatal medium spiny neurons

metadata.description: Neuropixels recording with electrical stimulation in an anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rat, 24 hours after 0.2 mg/kg LSD administration. Includes raw AP (30 kHz) and LFP (2.5 kHz) data, NIDQ auxiliary channels with stimulation triggers, and Kilosort4-sorted units with Phy curation.Electrode coordinates: Stereotaxic positions are provided per-contact in Paxinos-Watson convention (+x=anterior, +y=superior, +z=right) in mm relative to bregma. Probe insertion: AP=3.4 mm anterior, ML=0.75 mm left of midline, DV=6.0 mm ventral from brain surface (probe tip). Perpendicular insertion (0 deg angle). Per-contact lateral offsets from the NP1 staggered column layout (48 um total spread) are incorporated into the z (ML) coordinate.

name: Mapping Slow Speckle Dynamics to Probe Cellular Metabolic Activity In Vivo using Laser Speckle Contrast Imaging

metadata.name: Mapping Slow Speckle Dynamics to Probe Cellular Metabolic Activity In Vivo using Laser Speckle Contrast Imaging
metadata.citation: Long, Emily (2026) Mapping Slow Speckle Dynamics to Probe Cellular Metabolic Activity In Vivo using Laser Speckle Contrast Imaging (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001766/draft
metadata.description: Laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) is widely used to measure blood flow, but speckle fluctuations may also encode biologically meaningful dynamics beyond perfusion. Foundational studies in dynamic light scattering (DLS) and micro-optical coherence tomography (μOCT) have also demonstrated that slow coherent signal fluctuations can arise from energy-dependent intracellular motion in in vitro and ex vivo systems. Building upon these advances, recent work has shown that LSCI has the potential to detect slow speckle dynamics (SSD) correlated with cellular dynamics in vivo. However, the biophysical mechanisms underlying SSD in intact brain tissues remain insufficiently validated. Establishing a mechanistic bridge from controlled ex vivo and in vitro conditions to in vivo brain measurements is critical for translating speckle-based imaging beyond perfusion measurements to enable label-free assessment of cellular and metabolic activity in disease models.

name: Two-photon calcium imaging of responses to drifting gratings and natural movies in the mouse visual cortex and Spatial transcriptomics data

metadata.name: Two-photon calcium imaging of responses to drifting gratings and natural movies in the mouse visual cortex and Spatial transcriptomics data
metadata.citation: Zobeiri, Omid (2026) Two-photon calcium imaging of responses to drifting gratings and natural movies in the mouse visual cortex and Spatial transcriptomics data (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001778/draft
metadata.description: This dataset contains recordings of neural activity using two-photon calcium imaging from the mouse primary visual cortex (VISp). The mice had GCaMP8s expressed in their inhibitory interneurons (Slc32a1). The experiments were conducted using the same data collection pipeline as the Allen Brain Observatory (de Vries et al., Nature Neurosci., 2020), with an improved microscopy system that uses multi-plane imaging. A running wheel was used to track locomotion. The recordings were performed during two different visual stimulus sets: 1. STAGE_0: Natural movie clips. 2. STAGE_1: Drifting gratings presented as full-field stimuli (approximately 120x90 degrees of visual space). These gratings were presented in 8 directions, at 5 contrasts (5%, 10%, 20%, 40%, 80%), as well as 8 directions, and 5 temporal frequencies (0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 Hz). This dataset also includes the gene expression tables associated with subsets of neurons in the neural recording dataset. The gene expression was quantified using HCR (Hybridization Chain Reaction) method and multiple rounds of multichannel lightsheet imaging. Transcriptomic data in available in a Github repository: https://github.com/omidzobeiri/CellxGene_tables_U01_130907_DS001778

metadata.citation: Allen Institute; de Vries, Saskia; da Costa, Nuno; Buice, Michael; Collman, Forrest; Reid, Clay; Abbasi-Asl, Reza; Arkhipov, Anton; Bleckert, Adam; Bodor, Agnes; Brittain, Derrick; Buchanan, Joann; Bumbarger, Dan; Denman, Daniel; Elabaddy, Leila; Gouwens, Nathan; Joyce, Emily; Kapner, Dan; Kinn, Sam; Larkin, Josh; Lecoq, Jerome; Mahalingam, Gathathri; Millman, Daniel; Ouellette, Naveen; Scheider-Mizell, Casey; Seshamani, Sharmi; Takasaki, Kevin; Takeno, Mark; Torres, Russel; Vumbaco, David; Waters, Jack; Wyrick, David; Yin, Wenjing; Zhuang, Jun (2026) Allen Institute - V1 Deep Dive Golden Mouse (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001780/draft
metadata.description: This is the functional imaging of the V1 Deep Dive functional connectomics dataset. It consists of visual responses of neurons in the primary visual cortex (V1/VISp) of a mouse that was subsequently used for EM imaging and reconstruction. The functional and connectomic data has been co-registered. This dataset contains dense calcium imaging of excitatory neurons at the center of mouse V1. A Slc17a7-IRES2-Cre;Camk2a-tTA;Ai94 (GCaMP6s) mouse was imagined using both 2-photon and 3-photon imaging. Five columns were imaged, column 1 in the center and columns 2-5 at each of the four corners. For each column, five volumes were imaged using multi-plane 2-photon imaging. Six planes, separated by 16 um, were imaged per volume. These stacked volumes thus span from ~50 um to ~550 um below the surface. The central column was further imaged using single-plane 3-photon imaged at 25 um steps down to ~725um. During imaging, the awake mouse was head-fixed on a running disc and presented with a variety of visual stimuli including full-field and windowed drifting gratings, natural scenes, and natural movies.

name: Visual gamma stimulation causes prolonged enhancement of low-frequency blood flow oscillations across cortical regions in mice

metadata.name: Visual gamma stimulation causes prolonged enhancement of low-frequency blood flow oscillations across cortical regions in mice
metadata.citation: Tang, Rockwell (2026) Visual gamma stimulation causes prolonged enhancement of low-frequency blood flow oscillations across cortical regions in mice (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001790/draft
metadata.description:
Introduction
Gamma entrainment using sensory stimuli (GENUS) uses 40Hz-pulsed sensory stimuli to entrain neural activity in the gamma band (30-150Hz). However, the effect of GENUS on low-frequency vascular oscillations has not been fully explored. 
Objectives
The objective of this study is to elucidate the effect of GENUS on vasomotion in healthy mice and potential confounds for future application in disease studies.
Methods
Head-fixed, awake C57Bl/6 mice (n=18; 9M 9F) aged between 18 to 60 weeks were subjected to white light of either 40Hz visual flicker (GENUS), or constant stimulus (control). Blood flow was imaged using laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) before, during, immediately after 1 hour of stimulus, and 30min after the stimulus termination. 
Results
A linear mixed-effects model showed that GENUS enhanced the magnitude of 0.2-0.4Hz blood flow oscillations by 38% during stimulation and by 30% at 30 minutes after stimulation compared to control when controlled for age, sex, and other factors. The effect on vasomotion was distributed across many cortical regions not limited to visual areas and lasted beyond 24 hours post-stimulus. 
Conclusion
These results support the exploration of GENUS for increasing vasomotion in therapeutic contexts.

contributor.name: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H)

metadata.description:
This Dandiset contains acute in vivo electrophysiology recordings from anesthetized pigs acquired using high-density flexible microelectrode arrays placed around the optic nerve and, in selected experiments, over early visual cortex. Recordings were acquired with an Intan RHD system during visual stimulation paradigms including full-field light flashes and localized retinal stimulation. The dataset includes raw extracellular voltage recordings converted from Intan RHD format to NWB, stimulus timing/TTL channels when available, electrode/channel metadata, and subject/session metadata.

These recordings support analysis of visually evoked responses across the porcine visual pathway, including ON/OFF response dynamics, spatial recruitment across optic-nerve electrode arrays, low-frequency field components, high-frequency compound-action-potential-like activity, and signal transmission from the retina/optic nerve toward visual cortex. The data were collected as part of acute swine studies using high-channel-count neural interfaces for large-scale mapping of visual signal propagation.

Funding support: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, ARPA-H.

name: Investigating the role of pH in presynaptic homeostatic potentiation at the vertebrate neuromuscular junction

metadata.name: Investigating the role of pH in presynaptic homeostatic potentiation at the vertebrate neuromuscular junction
metadata.citation: Lindgren, Clark (2026) Investigating the role of pH in presynaptic homeostatic potentiation at the vertebrate neuromuscular junction (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001829/draft

contributor.name: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke

metadata.description: This project continues thirty-two years of research with undergraduate students at Grinnell College, who participate in all aspects of the research. My laboratory studies synaptic transmission at the vertebrate neuromuscular junction (NMJ). This project will focus on a phenomenon called presynaptic homeostatic potentiation (PHP), which refers to the increase in acetylcholine (ACh) release that follows the partial inhibition of postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors (AChRs). Although the phenomenon is well-known, the identity or nature of the feedback signal from the muscle to the nerve has eluded researchers for over 40 years. We recently uncovered evidence that protons in the synaptic cleft link the inhibition of AChRs to the increase in ACh release. Specifically, we found that (i) PHP requires functional acid sensing ion channels (ASICs), (ii) a decrease in extracellular pH increases quantal ACh release and precludes PHP, (iii) a strong extracellular pH buffer prevents PHP, (iv) inhibition of the plasma membrane Ca2+ ATPase (PMCA), which removes protons from the synaptic cleft, increases neurotransmitter release and precludes PHP, and (v) ASIC channels are present at the mouse NMJ. The projects described in this proposal will test the hypothesis that protons control PHP at the vertebrate NMJ. Our first goal is to measure the pH in the synaptic cleft of a mouse NMJ while the synapse is undergoing PHP. We plan to introduce fluorescent pH indicators into the cleft using AAV viruses. In preliminary experiments we successfully expressed pHusion-Ex and demonstrated its expected sensitivity to pH. We also plan to use a ‘kisser” probe that will allow us to examine pH in the immediate vicinity of the PMCA in the muscle membrane at the synapse. Our second goal is to understand the mechanism by which block of AChRs lowers the pH in the cleft. Our third goal is to characterize the effect of external pH in the synaptic cleft by applying acidic buffer via a micropipette and by photolytically uncaging NPE-caged-protons with a UV laser. Finally, we will explore PHP while allowing muscle action potentials to release internal calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. PHP has never been investigated under these physiological conditions. Collectively, these studies will expand our knowledge of pH in the synaptic cleft at the mammalian NMJ, especially while undergoing PHP. Since protons are increasingly recognized as signaling molecules at a variety of synapses, what we learn at the NMJ about gain adjustments effected by postsynaptic detection of neurotransmitter efficacy could represent a simple and widespread mechanism for maintaining effective communication across synapses throughout the central and peripheral nervous systems. In addition to its scientific merit, this grant will directly support at least 12 undergraduate students in consequential research experiences. Most of these students, if not all, will matriculate in a Ph.D. program in a biomedical science or medical school.
studyTarget: Presynaptic Homeostatic Potentiation

name: Cholinergic interneuron control of intrastriatal GABAergic circuits targeting spiny projection neurons is disrupted in Parkinson's disease models

metadata.name: Cholinergic interneuron control of intrastriatal GABAergic circuits targeting spiny projection neurons is disrupted in Parkinson's disease models
metadata.citation: Clarke, Vernon (2026) Cholinergic interneuron control of intrastriatal GABAergic circuits targeting spiny projection neurons is disrupted in Parkinson's disease models (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001832/draft
metadata.description: Cholinergic interneuron control of intrastriatal GABAergic circuits targeting spiny projection neurons is disrupted in Parkinson’s disease modelsBelal, M.1, Perez-Rosello, T.1, Guven E. B.4, Xie, Z.1, Li, J. 3, Dauer, W.2, Tkatch, T.1, Assous, M.5, Tepper, J.M.4 , Clarke, V. R. J.1 , Surmeier, D. J.1Affiliation:1-Department of Neuroscience, Feinberg School of Medicine, Northwestern University, Chicago, IL USA2- Peter O’Donnell Jr. Brain Institute, Departments of Neurology and Neuroscience, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX, USA.3 - Department of Internal Medicine, University of Michigan Medical School. Ann Arbor, MI, USA4- Molecular and Behavioral Neuroscience, Rutgers University, Newark, NJ USA5 - School of Biosciences, Cardiff University, Cardiff, UKParkinson’s disease (PD) is known to alter the intrinsic properties of striatal cholinergic interneurons (ChIs). However, how PD shapes ChI control of intrastriatal GABAergic circuits regulating principal spiny projection neurons (SPNs) is unknown. To fill this gap, striatal circuits in healthy and parkinsonian mice were interrogated. In ex vivo brain slices from healthy mice, optogenetic stimulation of ChIs evoked GABAA receptor currents in both indirect and direct pathway SPNs that were attributable to nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR)-mediated activation of GABAergic interneurons. Simulations suggested that this circuit exerts a state-dependent control of SPN dendritic integration that was modulated by concomitant muscarinic receptor signaling. Surprisingly, in mouse models of prodromal and parkinsonian states, the ability of ChIs to engage this intrastriatal circuitry was disrupted because interneurons down-regulated nicotinic AChRs. Taken together, these studies suggest that impaired ChI control of GABAergic interneurons contribute to behavioral deficits in both prodromal and clinical PD states.

name: CYP2C11 Overexpression Targeting Peri-lesion Astrocytes to Improve Cognitive Function in Mice with Multi-infarct Dementia

metadata.name: CYP2C11 Overexpression Targeting Peri-lesion Astrocytes to Improve Cognitive Function in Mice with Multi-infarct Dementia
metadata.citation: Liu, Zhongwu (2026) CYP2C11 Overexpression Targeting Peri-lesion Astrocytes to Improve Cognitive Function in Mice with Multi-infarct Dementia (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001833/draft
metadata.description: This project will explore a novel strategy to improve cognitive function in multi-infarct dementia mice via AAV-mediated overexpression of CYP2C11 targeting peri-lesion astrocytes. CYP2C11 is a key generating enzyme of epoxyeicosatrienoic acids (EETs). Localized EET production will promote vasodilation, increase cerebral blood flow (CBF) in hypoperfused regions, and suppress neuroinflammation, thereby potentially improving cognitive function. Thus, AAV-mediated astrocyte overexpression of CYP2C11 may provide therapeutic benefit for a large patient population with a significant unmet medical need of dementia therapy.

name: Injection of alpha-synuclein preformed fibrils into mouse M2 cortex impairs corticostriatal glutamate transmission compared to monomer injected mice

metadata.name: Injection of alpha-synuclein preformed fibrils into mouse M2 cortex impairs corticostriatal glutamate transmission compared to monomer injected mice
metadata.citation: Menard, Marissa (2026) Injection of alpha-synuclein preformed fibrils into mouse M2 cortex impairs corticostriatal glutamate transmission compared to monomer injected mice (Version draft) [Data set]. DANDI Archive. https://dandiarchive.org/dandiset/001835/draft
metadata.description: Ex-vivo slice recordings from mice co-injected with AAV8-hSyn-hChR2-tdTOMATO and either monomeric a-synuclein or synuclein preformed fibrils (PFFs) in the M2 cortex were assessed 6w post-injection via optogenetic stimulation of cortical afferents. This dataset includes data from whole--cell patch clamp recordings including spontaneous and evoked EPSCs, paired-pulse ratios, asynchronous synaptic events and intrinsic excitability. The data confirms there is a physiological difference between M2-PFF and M2-monomer injected animals as shown by a decrease in evoked excitatory current amplitude in the PFF-injected mice. The asynchronous release experiments showed fewer events in PFF-injected mice suggesting a reduction in active release sites.
